skill.name: pci-compliance
skill.description: Guides implementation of PCI DSS controls for payment systems, cardholder data handling, tokenization, encryption, access control, audit logging, and scope reduction.
skill.summary: Apply PCI DSS payment security controls with practical implementation guidance.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed against SKILL.md context. The reported command execution, sensitive-file, weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and Windows SAM matches are false positives caused by Markdown fences, PCI terminology, cookie settings, resource names, and defensive examples; no prompt injection or malicious behavior was found. A low-risk documentation concern remains because examples include placeholder payment secrets and card data that should not be copied into production.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Payment Secrets and Card Data
low_findings.description: The skill includes sample Stripe key and card values for demonstration. They appear to be placeholders and test data, but users could copy the pattern into production code instead of using managed secret storage and hosted collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are clearly shown inside instructional examples, not active code in this package. The risk is documentation misuse rather than credential theft or code execution.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The external command detections correspond to Markdown code fences around Python examples. No Ruby backtick execution, shell execution, subprocess invocation, or user-controlled command construction was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are fenced documentation examples. There is no executable script file in the reported file structure.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive and Reconnaissance Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The sensitive-file, Windows SAM, weak-crypto, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance alerts are false positives. They match PCI guidance terms, encryption key variables, cookie attributes, resource names, and defensive checklist language.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are compliance guidance and safe configuration examples. No evidence found of protected file access, host probing, network scanning, or weak cryptographic implementation.

content.user_title: Secure Payment Systems for PCI DSS
content.value_statement: Payment systems must protect cardholder data and reduce compliance scope. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code apply PCI DSS controls with practical implementation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains the 12 PCI DSS requirement areas in payment system context.
Provides guidance for avoiding storage of CVV, PIN, and track data.
Shows tokenization patterns for payment processor tokens and vault designs.
Outlines encryption, HTTPS, access control, and audit logging controls.
Maps common SAQ types to payment architecture choices.
Lists practical scope reduction methods for payment applications.
limitations: Does not replace a Qualified Security Assessor or formal PCI validation.
Examples require adaptation before use in production environments.
Does not include the referenced support files listed in the resources section.
May need updates for newer PCI DSS versions or processor-specific rules.

use_cases.title: Design a Payment Flow
use_cases.description: Review a planned checkout architecture and identify ways to keep card data away from application servers.

use_cases.title: Prepare for a PCI Review
use_cases.description: Create a control checklist for encryption, access control, logging, and prohibited data storage.

use_cases.title: Reduce Compliance Scope
use_cases.description: Compare hosted payments, tokenization, segmentation, and outsourcing options for a smaller PCI footprint.

prompt_templates.title: Review Basic Payment Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my payment flow for PCI DSS concerns. Focus on where card data enters, moves, and is stored.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a checkout design

prompt_templates.title: Create a Storage Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a PCI DSS cardholder data storage policy for my application. Include prohibited data, allowed data, masking, and retention rules.

prompt_templates.title: Map Controls to Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Map PCI DSS controls to this payment architecture. Cover tokenization, encryption, access control, logging, and vulnerability management.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Scope Reduction
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this payment system and propose a PCI scope reduction plan. Compare hosted payment pages, tokenization, segmentation, and processor delegation.

output_examples.input: Review a checkout that sends card data to the application server before charging through a processor.
output_examples.output: The review identifies direct cardholder data handling, recommends hosted collection or client-side tokenization, and lists controls still needed for logs, access, and transport security.

output_examples.input: Create a PCI DSS checklist for a team storing encrypted PAN values.
output: Confirm CVV, PIN, and track data are never stored.
Verify PAN encryption, key management, masking, and retention controls.
Check access control, audit logging, vulnerability management, and incident response evidence.

output_examples.input: Help decide between SAQ A, SAQ A-EP, and SAQ D for an online store.
output_examples.output: The response compares each SAQ type against how card data is collected, processed, and transmitted, then highlights architecture changes that may reduce scope.

best_practices: Use hosted payment pages or client-side tokenization when possible.
Store only the minimum cardholder data required for business needs.
Validate guidance with a PCI professional before production rollout.
anti_patterns: Do not store CVV, PIN, or magnetic stripe data after authorization.
Do not copy placeholder payment secrets into application source code.
Do not treat sample snippets as a complete compliance program.

faq.question: Does this skill make my system PCI compliant?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance, but formal PCI compliance requires evidence, assessment, and applicable validation.

faq.question: Can it help choose the right SAQ?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains common SAQ patterns and how payment architecture affects scope.

faq.question: Does it recommend storing card data?
faq.answer: No. It emphasizes minimizing storage and avoiding prohibited data such as CVV, PIN, and track data.

faq.question: Can I use the code examples directly?
faq.answer: Treat them as educational examples. Adapt them, add missing production controls, and review them before deployment.

faq.question: Does it cover encryption and tokenization?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers payment processor tokens, custom token vault concepts, AES-GCM examples, and HTTPS requirements.

faq.question: Is a PCI assessor still needed?
faq.answer: Yes. A Qualified Security Assessor or your acquiring bank can confirm the correct validation path.
